claim: Hybrid CSA/HCA attention compresses KV entries along sequence dimension, combines sparse selected compressed blocks with a local sliding-window branch, and enables 1M-token context support.
transfer: Primary Project X candidate: small dual-memory attention against transformer control.

claim: At 1M context, V4-Pro is reported to require 27% of DeepSeek-V3.2 single-token inference FLOPs and 10% of its KV cache; V4-Flash reports 10% FLOPs and 7% KV cache.
transfer: Track decode FLOPs and memory bytes as first-class metrics.

claim: Anticipatory Routing and SwiGLU clamping are used to handle MoE-related loss spikes and outliers.
transfer: Do not add MoE until memory experiment is stable; if routing is used, include spike detection and a kill condition.

claim: MLA jointly compresses keys and values into a latent vector, reducing inference KV cache while preserving MHA-like capability better than MQA/GQA in reported ablations.
transfer: Implement latent KV as a baseline between full attention and compressed sparse memory.

claim: DeepSeek V3 combines MLA, DeepSeekMoE, auxiliary-loss-free load balancing, MTP, FP8 training, and communication overlap at 671B total parameters.
transfer: Use as evidence that gains are system-level; avoid attributing all improvement to one module.

claim: Reasoning ability is primarily unlocked by large-scale RL/post-training on top of a V3-style base rather than by a new base architecture.
transfer: Keep reasoning post-training separate from base architecture experiments.

claim: NSA argues sparse attention must be natively trainable and hardware aligned, using coarse compression plus fine-grained selection to preserve global awareness and local precision.
transfer: Sparse memory selection must be block-contiguous and measured for IO, not only selected-token count.

claim: MLA shifts decode from memory-bound toward compute-bound regimes, with reuse/recompute variants trading compute for off-chip memory access.
transfer: Benchmark memory-access proxy for latent cache variants.

claim: Selective state-space models provide linear-time sequence modeling through input-dependent recurrent state updates.
transfer: Use SSM-like state as a cheap global summary, but not as the only memory for exact retrieval.

claim: Hybrid Transformer-Mamba stacks mix attention and SSM layers to balance recall and linear-time sequence processing.
transfer: Hybrid local attention plus recurrent/compressed memory is more plausible than pure SSM for Project X.

claim: Test-time memory models learn to memorize and retrieve information during inference rather than relying only on fixed context activations.
transfer: Candidate axis: explicit writable memory with learned surprise/write policy.

claim: Memory-centric long-context modeling treats memory construction/access as the central design object rather than an add-on cache trick.
transfer: Use memory quality metrics and not only perplexity.

claim: Mamba-like states can suffer long-range forgetting; memory pattern changes can improve retention and speed.
transfer: If using SSM memory, add retention probes and delayed-binding tests.

claim: Muon can replace AdamW for many hidden-layer matrices at LLM scale with memory-efficient distributed implementation, but optimizer choice can confound architecture evaluation.
transfer: Defer Muon until candidate beats control under AdamW.

claim: LongRoPE extends RoPE context windows with non-uniform interpolation/search and readjustment.
transfer: Useful for controls when context length, not memory architecture, is the bottleneck.

claim: YaRN extends RoPE context with efficient fine-tuning and no major runtime memory cost.
transfer: Use as simpler context-extension baseline later.

claim: Ring Attention distributes blockwise attention/FFN computation to remove per-device memory limits for very long sequences.
transfer: Not first experiment on one GPU; informs future scaling plan.

claim: GQA reduces KV-cache bandwidth versus MHA while retaining better quality than MQA after uptraining.
transfer: Include GQA-style control if full attention is too memory-heavy.

claim: IO-aware tiling reduces attention memory from quadratic materialization toward linear saved state and improves wall time.
transfer: Use existing kernels where possible; do not hand-roll slow attention and call it architecture.

claim: Fine-grained expert segmentation plus shared experts promotes expert specialization while preserving common knowledge paths.
transfer: MoE is a later compute-allocation experiment, not the first memory experiment.
